posts.title: The 'proprietary blend' is the cleanest tax on the people who can't afford a chemist.
posts.dek: Brands hide doses behind the phrase 'proprietary blend' because they want to charge clinical-grade prices for sub-clinical doses. The math behind why it works, and a side-by-side reveal of 14 pre-workout labels we ran through the lab last month.
posts.author: Adrian Kojima

posts.title: I visited four Costco supplement aisles. Here's the dosing spread.
posts.dek: The big-box channel has a different supply chain than DTC. Some of it is better. Most of it is worse. None of it is what you'd expect from the price tag.
posts.author: Mira J.

posts.title: Cost-per-clinical-serving — the only supplement metric that matters.
posts.dek: Why we don't compare on cost-per-scoop, cost-per-pill, or cost-per-bottle. The math of clinical-effect dosing, explained with worked examples from the whey, creatine, and magnesium categories.
posts.author: Adrian K.

posts.title: Why AG1 lost a full point in our Q1 re-test.
posts.dek: Greens powders are the hardest category we cover. We re-tested AG1 against our v3.2 rubric and the score dropped. Here's the chemistry behind it.
posts.author: Dr. Park

posts.title: FDA recalls three pre-workout brands for undisclosed DMAA derivatives.
posts.dek: The substance keeps reappearing in 'clean' brands. A short field guide to what to look for on the label, with structures.
posts.author: Adrian K.
